files | ## Repository files navigation # Agentic AI Investment Analysis Sample [Permalink: Agentic AI Investment Analysis Sample](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#agentic-ai-investment-analysis-sample) An advanced AI-powered investment analysis platform that leverages multi-agent workflows to perform comprehensive analysis of investment opportunities. This sample application demonstrates how to build sophisticated agentic AI systems using the [![Microsoft Agent Framework](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample/raw/main/_assets/agentframework.svg)](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample/blob/main/_assets/agentframework.svg)[Microsoft Agent Framework](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework), Azure services, and modern web technologies. ![InvestAI - Agentic AI Sample](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample/raw/main/_assets/InvestAI_screenshot.png) ## 📋 Overview [Permalink: 📋 Overview](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-overview) This application provides an intelligent system for analyzing investment opportunities through automated document processing, multi-agent analysis workflows, and interactive "what-if" scenario planning. The system orchestrates specialized AI agents that collaborate to provide comprehensive investment insights from multiple perspectives: financial, risk, market, and compliance. ## ✨ Key Features [Permalink: ✨ Key Features](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-key-features) - **Multi-Agent Investment Analysis**: Orchestrates specialized AI agents (Financial Analyst, Risk Analyst, Market Analyst, Compliance Analyst) that work together to provide comprehensive investment analysis - **Document Intelligence**: Automated processing and analysis of investment documents (financial statements, pitch decks, market research, etc.) - **Real-Time Streaming**: Server-Sent Events (SSE) architecture for live streaming of agent progress and analysis results - **Interactive What-If Scenarios**: Chat-based interface for exploring hypothetical scenarios and getting instant analysis - **Workflow Visualization**: Visual representation of agent workflows and their execution status - **Investment Opportunity Management**: Full CRUD operations for managing investment opportunities and their associated documents ## 🏗️ Architecture [Permalink: 🏗️ Architecture](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#%EF%B8%8F-architecture) ### Main Components [Permalink: Main Components](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#main-components) #### 1\. **Backend API (`api-app/`)** [Permalink: 1. Backend API (api-app/)](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#1-backend-api-api-app) A FastAPI-based REST API that serves as the orchestration layer for the AI agents and data management. **Core Technologies:** - **FastAPI**: Modern, high-performance web framework - **Microsoft Agent Framework**: Powers the multi-agent workflow orchestration - **Azure Cosmos DB**: NoSQL database for storing opportunities, analyses, and documents - **Azure Blob Storage**: Document storage and retrieval - **Azure OpenAI**: LLM provider for AI agent capabilities **Key Modules:** - **Workflow System** (`app/workflow/`): - `investment_workflow.py`: Main investment analysis workflow orchestrating multiple specialized agents - `investment_executors.py`: Individual agent implementations (Financial, Risk, Market, Compliance analysts) - `prompts/`: Agent-specific prompt templates for consistent analysis - **What-If Chat System** (`app/what_if_chat/`): - `what_if_workflow.py`: Interactive chat workflow for scenario exploration - `what_if_executors.py`: Specialized agents for handling hypothetical scenarios - **Services** (`app/services/`): - `analysis_workflow_executor_service.py`: Manages workflow execution and event streaming - `document_processing_service.py`: Handles multi-stage document processing - `analysis_service.py`: CRUD operations for investment analyses - `opportunity_service.py`: Management of investment opportunities - **API Routers** (`app/routers/`): - `analysis.py`: Endpoints for starting/monitoring analyses with SSE streaming - `chat.py`: Interactive chat interface for what-if scenarios - `opportunity.py`: Investment opportunity management endpoints - **Data Models** (`app/models/`): - Pydantic models for type-safe data handling - Document, Opportunity, Analysis, User models #### 2\. **Frontend Web Application (`web-app/`)** [Permalink: 2. Frontend Web Application (web-app/)](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#2-frontend-web-application-web-app) A modern React-based single-page application providing an intuitive user interface. **Core Technologies:** - **React 18** with TypeScript - **Vite**: Fast build tool and dev server - **Shadcn/ui**: Beautiful, accessible UI component library - **Tailwind CSS**: Utility-first CSS framework - **Lucide React**: Icon library **Key Features:** - Dashboard for managing investment opportunities - Document upload and processing interface - Real-time analysis progress visualization with agent workflow diagrams - Interactive chat interface for what-if scenarios - Responsive design for desktop and mobile **Main Pages:** - `Index.tsx`: Landing page with feature showcase - `NewOpportunity.tsx` / `EditOpportunity.tsx`: Opportunity management - `ProcessDocuments.tsx`: Document upload and processing - `Analysis.tsx`: Analysis execution and visualization #### 3\. **AI Agent Workflow** [Permalink: 3. AI Agent Workflow](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#3-ai-agent-workflow) The system uses a sophisticated multi-agent architecture: **Investment Analysis Workflow:** ``` Data Preparation → [Financial Analyst, Risk Analyst, Market Analyst, Compliance Analyst] → Analysis Aggregator → Investment Debate Executor → Summary Report Generator ``` Each agent: - Has specialized knowledge and analysis focus - Receives contextual prompts from prompt templates - Processes in parallel where possible (fan-out pattern) - Results are aggregated and synthesized - Agents engage in a debate to challenge assumptions - Final summary report is generated **What-If Chat Workflow:** ``` Planning Agent → [Financial, Risk, Market, Compliance Agents] → Analysis Summarizer ``` - Interprets user questions and scenarios - Routes to relevant specialist agents - Synthesizes responses into coherent answers - Maintains conversation context across turns #### 4\. **Event Streaming Architecture** [Permalink: 4. Event Streaming Architecture](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#4-event-streaming-architecture) The application uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time updates: - **Event Queue System** (`app/utils/sse_stream_event_queue.py`): In-memory queue for managing events per analysis - **Event Types**: workflow\_started, agent\_started, agent\_progress, agent\_completed, workflow\_completed - **Client Subscription**: Frontend subscribes to `/api/analysis/{id}/events` endpoint - **Historical Events**: New subscribers receive historical events before live updates - **Background Processing**: Analysis runs in FastAPI background tasks while streaming events ## 📦 Prerequisites [Permalink: 📦 Prerequisites](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-prerequisites) - **Python 3.11+** (3.13 recommended) - **Node.js 18+** and npm - **Azure Account**with: - Azure Cosmos DB instance - Azure Blob Storage account - Azure OpenAI service with a deployed chat model - **Environment Variables**: See Configuration section below ## ⚙️ Configuration [Permalink: ⚙️ Configuration](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#%EF%B8%8F-configuration) ### Backend Environment Variables [Permalink: Backend Environment Variables](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#backend-environment-variables) Create a `.env` file in the `api-app/` directory: ``` # API Server Settings API_SERVER_HOST=0.0.0.0 API_SERVER_PORT=8084 API_SERVER_WORKERS=1 LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG DEBUG=True ENVIRONMENT=development # Azure Cosmos DB COSMOS_DB_ENDPOINT=https://.documents.azure.com:443/ COSMOS_DB_DATABASE_NAME=ai-investment-analysis-sample # Azure Blob Storage AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME=your-storage-account AZURE_STORAGE_CONTAINER_NAME=opportunity-documents # Azure OpenAI AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT=https://.cognitiveservices.azure.com/openai/deployments//chat/completions?api-version=2025-01-01-preview AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME= ``` ### Frontend Environment Variables [Permalink: Frontend Environment Variables](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#frontend-environment-variables) Create a `.env` file in the `web-app/` directory: ``` VITE_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8084/api ``` ## 🚀 Running Locally [Permalink: 🚀 Running Locally](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-running-locally) ### 1\. Backend API Setup [Permalink: 1. Backend API Setup](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#1-backend-api-setup) ``` # Navigate to the api-app directory cd api-app # Create and activate a virtual environment (recommended) python -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate # Install dependencies pip install -r requirements.txt # Ensure your .env file is configured (see Configuration section) # Run the FastAPI server python main.py # The API will be available at http://localhost:8084 # API documentation at http://localhost:8084/docs ``` **Alternative: Run with uvicorn directly** ``` uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8084 --reload ``` ### 2\. Frontend Web Application Setup [Permalink: 2. Frontend Web Application Setup](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#2-frontend-web-application-setup) Open a new terminal window: ``` # Navigate to the web-app directory cd web-app # Install dependencies npm install # Ensure your .env file is configured (see Configuration section) # Start the development server npm run dev # The web app will be available at http://localhost:8080 ``` ### 3\. Azure Services Setup [Permalink: 3. Azure Services Setup](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#3-azure-services-setup) **Cosmos DB:** 1. Create a Cosmos DB account with NoSQL API 2. The application will automatically create the database and containers on first run 3. Ensure your connection endpoint is in the `.env` file 4. Ensure proper access permissions on Cosmos DB account: Follow the steps in this article: [Connect to Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL using role-based access control and Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/nosql/how-to-connect-role-based-access-control?pivots=azure-cli) **Blob Storage:** 1. Create a Storage Account 2. Create a container named `opportunity-documents` (or your chosen name) 3. Ensure proper access permissions (Storage Blob Data Contributor role) **Azure AI Foundry:** 1. Deploy an Azure AI Foundry resource - or connect directly to an existing one if available 2. Deploy a chat model (e.g., GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-mini) 3. Note the endpoint and deployment name 4. Ensure proper access permissions (Azure AI User role) ### 4\. Authentication [Permalink: 4. Authentication](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#4-authentication) For local development with Azure services, authenticate using Azure CLI: ``` az login ``` The application uses `DefaultAzureCredential` which supports: - Azure CLI authentication (for local development) - Managed Identity (for Azure deployments) - Environment variables - Interactive browser authentication ## 💡 Using the Application [Permalink: 💡 Using the Application](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-using-the-application) ### 1\. Create an Investment Opportunity [Permalink: 1. Create an Investment Opportunity](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#1-create-an-investment-opportunity) - Navigate to the home page - Click "New Opportunity" or go to `/opportunity/new` - Fill in the investment details: - Company name - Investment hypothesis - Investment stage - Industry - Initial investment amount - Description ### 2\. Process Documents [Permalink: 2. Process Documents](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#2-process-documents) - After creating an opportunity, upload relevant documents: - Financial statements - Pitch decks - Market research - Due diligence documents - The system will automatically extract and analyze the content - Monitor real-time processing progress ### 3\. Run Investment Analysis [Permalink: 3. Run Investment Analysis](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#3-run-investment-analysis) - From the opportunity detail page, click "Start Analysis" - Watch as specialized AI agents analyze the opportunity: - Data preparation and context loading - Parallel analysis by Financial, Risk, Market, and Compliance agents - Agent debate to challenge assumptions - Final summary report generation - View real-time progress and intermediate results via SSE streaming ### 4\. Explore What-If Scenarios [Permalink: 4. Explore What-If Scenarios](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#4-explore-what-if-scenarios) - After analysis completes, use the chat interface - Ask hypothetical questions like: - "What if the market growth is 10% slower?" - "How would a 20% increase in costs affect the ROI?" - "What are the compliance risks if we expand to Europe?" - Get instant analysis from relevant specialist agents ## 🛠️ Technology Stack Summary [Permalink: 🛠️ Technology Stack Summary](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#%EF%B8%8F-technology-stack-summary) **Backend:** - FastAPI 0.116+ - Microsoft Agent Framework - Azure Cosmos DB SDK - Azure Blob Storage SDK - Azure OpenAI SDK - Pydantic 2.11+ - Uvicorn (ASGI server) **Frontend:** - React 18 - TypeScript - Vite - Shadcn/ui + Radix UI - Tailwind CSS - React Router **AI:** - [Microsoft Agent Framework for multi-agent orchestration](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework) - Azure OpenAI (GPT-4.1/GPT-5) - Custom agent executors with specialized prompts **Infrastructure:** - Azure Cosmos DB (NoSQL) - Azure Blob Storage - Azure OpenAI Service - Azure Entra ID (authentication) ## 🔧 Troubleshooting [Permalink: 🔧 Troubleshooting](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-troubleshooting) ### Backend Issues [Permalink: Backend Issues](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#backend-issues) **Cosmos DB Connection Errors:** - Verify `COSMOS_DB_ENDPOINT` is correct - Check Azure CLI authentication: `az login` - Verify firewall rules allow your IP - Ensure proper RBAC roles assigned **Agent Framework Import Errors:** - Ensure `agent-framework` is installed: `pip install agent-framework` - Check Python version is 3.11+ **OpenAI API Errors:** - Verify deployment name matches your Azure OpenAI deployment - Check API quotas and rate limits - Ensure proper authentication ### Frontend Issues [Permalink: Frontend Issues](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#frontend-issues) **API Connection Errors:** - Verify `VITE_API_BASE_URL` points to correct backend URL - Check CORS settings in backend `.env` - Ensure backend is running ## 🤝 Contributing [Permalink: 🤝 Contributing](https://github.com/Azure-Samples/Agentic-AI-Investment-Analysis-Sample#-contributing) This is a sample application designed to demonstrate agentic AI patterns. # Building an Open-Source Agentic AI Equity Research Tool

[![Richard Goerwitz](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*PcKY3d6N2MsIN_u3_ia9Dw.jpeg)](https://achaar.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--172783ed6961---------------------------------------)

[Richard Goerwitz](https://achaar.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--172783ed6961---------------------------------------)

Follow

12 min read

·

Dec 1, 2025

46

3

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D172783ed6961&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.datadriveninvestor.com%2Fbuilding-an-open-source-agentic-ai-equity-research-tool-172783ed6961&source=---header_actions--172783ed6961---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*_8yNngBs5JQKhWA7DWFa-Q.png)

I spent two months building an agentic AI research tool that screens international stocks. It costs relatively little to run, rejects around 90% of already pre-screened candidate equities, and allows me to focus more energy on the ones I really care about.

This article details how the agentic AI system works, what it does well, where it struggles, and what you should know before building something similar.

The code is open source: [https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent) (sample [analysis](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent/blob/main/README-1681-HK-2025-12-01.md); overview on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7400775746727792640/)).

## The Problem

By day I work in data engineering and information security.

Lately, though, I’ve spent my evenings thinking about ways US investors worried about deficits, AI bubbles, and general political instability can hedge those risks by diversifying into mid-cap stocks homed in ex-US markets— markets like Tokyo, Hong Kong, Warsaw, and Kuala Lumpur, where perfectly legitimate, growing, small and mid-cap companies trade and make money without heavy dependence on US markets and trade.

Because equities matching this thesis are often not well covered by US analysts, finding and evaluating them can be hard and takes time that I don’t have. It costs money, too. Bloomberg Terminal is ~$20,000/year. FactSet is just as bad. Yes, you can abandon your portfolio to managers like Fisher, but they will take a cut in return.

With AI now opening up new possibilities, I wondered: Could I build an agentic AI equity research tool on the cheap to help me find and screen equities that fit my thesis and generate easy alpha?

## The Solution I Built

The equity evaluator I ended up building uses LangGraph (an agent orchestration framework) to run a five-phase workflow.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*0lH5NVyc9FUYibKD1RvT-Q.png)

Complete Agentic Graph-Workflow

**Phase 1: Data Gathering (Parallel)**

Four specialist agents run simultaneously:

- **Market Analyst**: price history, liquidity, RSI/MACD/Bollinger
- **Fundamentals Analyst**: P/E, ROE, D/E, cash flow, etc.
- **News Analyst**: recent filings, company news (Tavily)
- **Sentiment Analyst**: retail buzz (StockTwits, Reddit)

This works well for Japan, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and other mature exchanges. It works poorly for Malaysia, Indonesia, and much of Southeast Asia because the free data sources don’t have solid data for listings on exchanges in those regions.

Here’s the actual fallback logic:

```
def get_financial_metrics(ticker: str) -> dict:
    """Try multiple sources, merge results, fill critical gaps."""
    result = {}

    # Primary: yfinance (free, usually good for liquid stocks)
    result = fetch_yfinance(ticker)

    # If key metrics missing, try yahooquery
    if missing_critical_fields(result):
        yahoo_data = fetch_yahooquery(ticker)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, yahoo_data)

    # Still gaps? Try FMP (if API key available)
    if missing_critical_fields(result) and FMP_API_KEY:
        fmp_data = fetch_fmp(ticker)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, fmp_data)

    # Last resort: web search for specific missing fields
    if missing_critical_fields(result):
        web_data = search_for_gaps(ticker, result)
        result = merge_keeping_best(result, web_data)

    return sanitize_and_validate(result)
```

The key problem: Searching for some stocks, particularly on exchanges in countries still new to the idea of standard accounting and transparency requirements, returns empty result sets. When that happens, subsequent agents have less to work with.

**Phase 2: Synthesis**

A Research Manager does the best it can with all four reports and writes a summary. In early versions of my code, the output of this stage was a 2,000-plus-word essay. Untethered LLMs can be quite verbose. I reduced the length to a few bullet points through aggressive prompt engineering (“DO NOT write introductory preambles”).

**Phase 3: Bull/Bear Debate (The Transformative Part)**

In this stage, two adversarial agents argue for 1–2 rounds. The Bull gets a prompt that tells it to “argue the STRONGEST case for buying — push back on Bear’s concerns.” The Bear gets instructed to “argue the STRONGEST case for avoiding — and to push back on Bull’s optimism.”

Example from HSBC (0005.HK):

**Bull**: “Trading at P/E 14.79, below historical average. 40% operating margin. $61B in free cash flow. This is cheap, not broken.”

**Bear**: “Revenue growth 4.8% is anemic. EPS declined 16.6% YoY. Operating in Hong Kong during geopolitical uncertainty. Mature bank with no growth story. Hard pass.”

The Portfolio Manager reads both arguments and applies the scoring rules. HSBC in the above case failed the growth requirement → SELL.

This debate-based evaluation structure is better than asking a single LLM to “analyze this stock.” Single agents hedge and compromise. They can also glom onto unintentionally leading language in your prompts. Adversarial agents commit them to different positions and allow subsequent agents to see all aspects of the debate clearly.

**Phase 4: Risk Sizing**

Once the Research Manager is finished synthesizing its inputs, three risk analysts (Conservative, Neutral, and Aggressive) then recommend a position size. Even with prodding, I found that the “Aggressive” one could be pretty cautious — Gemini seems trained to be risk-averse. But that’s OK with me. I’m cautious as well.

**Final Decision**

At this stage, the Portfolio Manager sets up hard gates:

- Financial health rating ≥ 50%
- Growth score ≥ 50%
- Liquidity ≥ $500k daily
- Analyst coverage < 15 (prefer undiscovered names)

HSBC, for example, as of my 2025–11–30 run scored 62.5% on health, 0% on growth → automatic SELL. Samsung scored fine on valuation, but had 100+ analysts talking about it → SELL.

That’s the idea, of course. The system enforces rules I care about, and surfaces only things that don’t break the rules. It saves me time.

In cases like the above, the logic looks like this:

```
def make_decision(health_score, growth_score, liquidity, coverage):
    # Hard fails - any one disqualifies the stock
    if health_score < 0.50:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Financial health < 50%"

    if growth_score < 0.50:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Growth score < 50%"

    if liquidity < 500_000:
        return "SELL", "Hard fail: Liquidity < $500k"

    if coverage >= 15:
        return "SELL", "Too much analyst coverage (not undiscovered)"

    # Passes all gates - now check soft factors (valuation, risks)
    return assess_qualitative_factors(...)
```

No amount of debate changes these thresholds. If growth = 0%, it’s a SELL.

## Why This Works

**1\. It Enforces Discipline** HSBC looks cheap at P/E 14.79. It’s HSBC — global bank, household name, pays a dividend. A human analyst might think, “But it’s HSBC. I listen to their Banyan Tree podcast. They’re great!” The AI doesn’t care. Growth score: 0%. Rejected.

Would I have rejected HSBC without the system? I don’t know. It might not have even occurred to me to look. Was the system right? Yes, probably. If I’m concerned, I have all the data I need in the report to allow me to start checking.

**2\. The Bull/Bear Structure Forces Deeper Thought** Single-LLM responses are bland and hedged. Multi-agent debate forces each side to commit. It’s the difference between a Wikipedia article (balanced but boring) and a courtroom debate (two strong cases with open advocacy and debate).

**3\. Full Audit Trail** Every data point has a source. Every score shows its calculation. When the system says “Health Score: 62.5% (5/12 raw, adjusted for 8 available points)”, you can see:

- Which 5 points it earned
- Which 7 points were N/A (and why)
- The exact math

Trust comes from being able to verify. Downstream AI agents get to see the work, and so does the user looking at the final report.

**4\. Zero Marginal Cost** After building it, running 100 stocks on Gemini’s free tier costs $0 but the 15 requests per minute limit makes things slow for batch jobs with more than a few tickers. I upgraded to paid second tier for faster batch processing and more generous API limits, and 300 stocks now costs me about $25.

Compare: Bloomberg Terminal is ~$2k/month.

## What Fails Reliably

**1\. Bad Data Contaminates Downstream Results** Case study: YTLPOWR.KL (YTL Power International, Malaysia)

The system on the last run (late Nov 2025) returned:

- P/E Ratio: 161,103,004,000.00 (yes, 161 billion)
- P/B Ratio: 27,230,309,079.61 (27 billion)
- Financial Health Score: 0% (all metrics N/A)

Is YTL Power a bad company? I have no idea. The free APIs don’t cover Malaysian stocks. yfinance scraped corrupted data, FMP returned empty responses, EODHD had nothing on that run (possibly another bug, but most other equities ran just fine).

The system correctly flagged these as anomalous and rejected the stock. But it rejected it for _data gaps_, not because of poor fundamentals. I learned nothing about the actual company.

**Lesson**: Free APIs work well for mature markets. For emerging markets (Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and many others), you’ll either need paid sources or you’ll have to treat results as low-confidence flags for manual research.

**2\. The System Is Verbose** Early reports were 2,000+ words. For every stock. The Bull agent would write:

“Based on comprehensive analysis of the financial statements, considering both quantitative metrics and qualitative factors, while acknowledging the inherent uncertainty in forward-looking projections…”

I had to add prompts like:

- “DO NOT write introductory preambles”
- “DO NOT summarize your summary”
- “USE bullet points, not paragraphs”
- “Maximum 300 words per response”

Even with these constraints, reports are still 800+ words. LLMs love to hear themselves talk.

**3\. Mega-Caps Sometimes Sneak Through** The thesis: find “undiscovered” stocks (analyst coverage < 15).

This works for mid-caps. For mega-caps the searches aren’t always as helpful:

**Tencent (0700.HK)**: “$400B market cap… but only 12 analysts tracked by yfinance… must be undiscovered!”

Obviously Tencent is not undiscovered. The analyst count is just an artifact of how I’m counting (English-language analysts tracked by yfinance).

**Fix**: I added a market cap threshold (>$50B = automatic “high coverage” assumption). But this is a patch, not a solution. The real issue: the system favors mega-caps because you can _find data on them_. Truly obscure mid-caps in Malaysia exist in data deserts at the moment, and my equity evaluator struggles with them.

**4\. Language Barriers Are Insurmountable** For Korean, Japanese, or Chinese stocks, local sentiment matters. But:

- StockTwits: English-only, US-centric
- Reddit: Mostly English
- Local forums: Require API access and language skills I don’t have

Result: the “Market Sentiment” section for most ex-US stocks says “Unable to assess local sentiment.”

If Samsung is getting roasted on Korean forums, the AI agents may not know about it unless I check manually (which defeats the purpose of having AI agents do the work).

**4\. Memory Isolation Took Longer Than It Should Have** Early versions showed a persistent bug where analyzing Canon would contaminate HSBC’s analysis. The AI would reference Canon’s chip shortage when discussing HSBC (a bank).

The fix:

```
# Before: Global memory (wrong)
bull_memory = ChromaDB(collection="bull_analysis")  # Shared across all tickers
bear_memory = ChromaDB(collection="bear_analysis")
```

```
# After: Ticker-specific namespaces (correct)
def create_memories(ticker: str):
    safe_name = ticker.replace(".", "_")  # 0005.HK → 0005_HK
    return {
        "bull": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_bull"),
        "bear": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_bear"),
        "risk": ChromaDB(collection=f"{safe_name}_risk")
    }
```

Each ticker now gets isolated memory. Canon’s analysis doesn’t leak into HSBC’s. If you write a system like this, pay close attention to what gets saved and how, and to how and when data gets cleared as it’s generated by the various nodes in the agent graph.

**5\. Speed Is Not a Feature** For 300 tickers:

- Quick mode: 6–12 hours
- Standard mode: 16–30 hours (varied as the code evolved)

Even on quick mode, this is slow. A human with Bloomberg can scan 50 tickers in 30 minutes, and do a passable job.

The trade-off: the AI is thorough (maybe too thorough), but at the moment it’s not fast.

## What I’d Do Differently (Advice)

**1\. Better Data Sources (Optional Future Extension)** I spent 60% of my time on prompt engineering. I should’ve spent 60% finding better data sources.

The difference between “good” and “great” analysis is data quality, not agent architecture.

**For those who want to extend this**: The system is designed to work with free APIs to keep it accessible. But if you’re targeting emerging markets seriously, paid sources would help:

- Polygon.io (international coverage)
- Koyfin ($20/mo for non-professionals)
- Alpha Vantage premium
- Company IR website scrapers (build your own)

The goal is to keep the core system free and open. Paid data should be pluggable extensions, not requirements. If you build connectors for these sources, please, create a pull request — but make sure they’re optional dependencies. Don’t forget to write tests, too, for everything (bad or malicious inputs, broken APIs, dead network connections, timeouts, rate limits, exception handling, and all the things).

**2\. Add a Data Quality Check Before Debate** Before the Bull/Bear debate, add an agent that says:

“8/12 financial metrics available. Data quality: GOOD. Proceed.”

OR

“2/12 financial metrics available. Data quality: POOR. Flag for manual review.”

This would’ve saved me from analyzing YTL Power (the stock with corrupted P/E data of 161 billion).

Implementation:

```
def check_data_quality(metrics: dict) -> str:
    total_fields = 12  # P/E, ROE, ROA, D/E, Revenue, EPS, etc.
    available = sum(1 for v in metrics.values() if v is not None)
    coverage = available / total_fields

    if coverage >= 0.66:  # 8+ of 12 fields
        return "GOOD"
    elif coverage >= 0.33:  # 4-7 of 12 fields
        return "MARGINAL"
    else:  # < 4 fields
        return "POOR"
```

```
# Then in the main flow:
data_quality = check_data_quality(financial_metrics)
if data_quality == "POOR":
    return {
        "decision": "REJECT",
        "reason": "Insufficient data for analysis",
        "metrics_available": f"{available}/{total_fields}"
    }
```

Run this before wasting compute on worthless debates.

**3\. Use Structured Outputs (When Filtering at Scale)** My agents return markdown text. This is human-readable and works well when you’re doing manual research on individual stocks.

**For batch filtering** (screening 300 stocks to find 15 BUYs), I’d love it if someone would add JSON output as an option:

```
{
  "ticker": "0005.HK",
  "company": "HSBC Holdings",
  "decision": "SELL",
  "conviction": "HIGH",
  "health_score": 62.5,
  "growth_score": 0.0,
  "position_size_pct": 0,
  "hard_fails": ["growth_score"],
  "key_risks": ["declining_earnings", "geopolitical_exposure"],
  "rationale": "Passes health gate but fails growth requirement (0% vs 50% threshold)"
}
```

Then we can filter programmatically: `jq '.[] | select(.decision == "BUY")' results.json`.

Keep markdown for detailed reports. Add JSON for programmatic filtering. Both have their place.

**4\. Pick Your Markets Carefully** I wanted a universal ex-US equity analyzer. I learned: there’s no such thing.

- Hong Kong/Japan/Taiwan: Excellent API coverage, system works
- Malaysia/Indonesia/Thailand: Data desert, system struggles
- Korea: Middle ground (decent data, language barrier)
- Most of Europe: solid
- India, Vietnam, Brazil: No access to US retail investors

**Lesson**: Build for the markets you trade. Don’t boil the ocean.

## Is This Useful?

**What this system does well**:

- Filters 300 candidates down to a dozen worth investigating
- Catches many value traps (cheap for a reason)
- Enforces discipline (hard gates prevent emotional trades)
- Costs $0–10 a run instead of $24,000/year

**What it doesn’t do**:

- Replace human judgment (it can’t)
- Work with bad data (garbage in, garbage out)
- Handle emerging markets without paid APIs
- Make real-time trading decisions (it’s too slow)

Think of it like this: if you were hiring an intern to do initial stock screening, this system does that intern’s job. It won’t replace a senior analyst, and it won’t find opportunities that aren’t in the data. But it will save you from wasting time on obvious rejects.

## The Code Is Open

Repository: [https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent](https://github.com/rgoerwit/ai-investment-agent)

**What’s included**:

- Full LangGraph workflow (6 agent types)
- Multi-source data fetcher (yfinance, FMP, EODHD, Tavily)
- 450+ unit tests
- Detailed setup instructions

**Requirements**:

- Python 3.12+
- Google Gemini API key (free tier works)
- Basic command-line skills
- Optional: Tavily, FMP, EODHD keys for better data

**What you can do with it**:

- Screen your own watchlist
- Modify the thesis (change thresholds, add/remove rules)
- Swap in better data sources (please do, but make them optional)
- Study how multi-agent systems work in practice

**What you shouldn’t do**:

- Trust it blindly with real money
- Expect perfection on day one
- Blame me when a recommendation doesn’t work out

## Final Thoughts

The real value of the agentic AI stock evaluator I built isn’t in finding the next 10-bagger. It’s in having a system that says “no” to 285 stocks so I can spend my time on the 15 that matter. That’s worth $100/month (which is what I spent one month).

If you’re building something similar, my advice is: Spend your time on data quality first, and add data confidence checks before you waste compute on garbage inputs. If I were to write it again, I might use structured outputs instead of markdown.

For emerging markets, consider building paid API connectors — but keep them optional so the system remains accessible to everyone at little base cost.

And if you find better free data sources for Malaysia, Indonesia, or Thailand, and other markets with weaker transparency standards, please create a pull request. I’m still looking.

# Building A Multi-Agent Investment Platform: From Research to Trade Execution

## Using Google ADK, A2A And MCP To Build And Design A Multi-Agent Investment System

[![Farhad Malik](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*7MhP4LsHsl3G_oE3LBVlsg.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@farhadmalik?source=post_page---byline--7ca297595ce9---------------------------------------)

[Farhad Malik](https://medium.com/@farhadmalik?source=post_page---byline--7ca297595ce9---------------------------------------)

Share

[Non medium members can use this link to read the article](https://medium.com/@farhadmalik/building-a-multi-agent-investment-platform-from-research-to-trade-execution-7ca297595ce9?sk=eddb1f0d305cf3d7ab6481750e23492d)

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:651/1*MWhaaCWXzGDhhVdOhvaHPg.png)

Intro image of the article

## Article Aim

This article explores how multiple specialist agents can be assembled to perform a single task. It provides an overview of multi-agent orchestration, communication protocols, and tool boundaries to build a scalable agentic investment system.

The system implements a five-agent architecture for investment management, where specialised AI agents collaborate through Google ADK to execute investment workflows with clear accountability and security boundaries.

This article is technical in nature. I will be explaining the technical core concepts and foundations throughout the article.

In particular, the article will focus on the following 6 areas:

1. High-level overview of the investment process
2. Explaining the core concepts of Tools, Prompts, and Memory as the three building blocks of each agent
3. Introducing MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) communication
4. Walking through the key architecture design decisions, including Google ADK integration and SOLID design principles
5. Deep dive into the code
6. Introducing the Portfolio Manager Orchestrator and how it manages the end-to-end workflow, along with a comparison of multi-agent orchestration patterns

The code can be downloaded from the GitHub repo [here](https://github.com/fintechexplained/multiagent_investment/tree/main).

_This article is part of the “Agentic AI For Investment Management” series. The remaining articles in the series are listed at the end._

_Disclaimer: This article reflects my personal views, and it is written for educational purposes only; readers should seek guidance from qualified professional advisors before making any investment decisions._
